trajectory_summary: The call moves in a single compressed arc: warm inbound → brief credit uncertainty → acceptance → booking. There is no resistance to overcome and no energy to build — Tim arrives ready. The booking is real, but the structural work underneath it is thin: no intro beat, no verify completion, no bill anchor, and a one-point credit gap that reaches the closer undocumented.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (6PM tomorrow), so a 'booked' outcome tag would be accurate. However, the appointment is structurally fragile: the credit threshold is at the edge of policy, no co-owner status was confirmed, no bill was collected, and the button-up protocol was not completed (no 'at your computer,' no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise). The booking is real; its durability is not established.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol completion on recycled warm leads
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Alright. K. Awesome. Yeah. We're planning on fixing tomorrow, and then I mean, you went through it all before. But I guess since they already went through it, they'll just make sure your credit's good at it. Pressure and Yeah. Go through it. But it should probably be about forty minutes is also.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter has a warm, cooperative customer who is ready to book — the easiest possible conditions for a clean button-up — and still skips four of six required elements. The 'they already went through it' logic is doing double duty here: it is Carter's justification for skipping both verify and button-up. If this habit holds on easy calls, it will cost sits on harder ones. The manager should drill the button-up checklist against this specific close and ask Carter to identify each missing element by name.

key_moments.quote: Hi, Carter. My name is Tim. I just missed a call from IIM Solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tim misnames the company on the inbound call and Carter does not correct it. The intro beat — which exists specifically to establish legitimacy and answer 'is this a scam?' — never occurs. Carter moves directly into record-searching. On a recycled lead with a warm customer this may feel low-stakes, but the codex is explicit: legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a required beat regardless of lead type.

Carter correctly states the threshold as 670 when Tim approximates it. One minute later Tim reports 669 and Carter accepts it. The codex flags a known discrepancy — SESSION18_fcbf33c1 rep stated 650, schema says 670 — and the policy question of whether the setter should DQ on stated credit or pass to the closer's pull is unresolved. Carter's acceptance of 669 may be within policy if the rule is 'let the closer's pull decide.' What is not ambiguous: Carter does not flag the one-point gap for the closer. The closer walks in blind to a potential credit issue.

key_moments.quote: That's fine. That was great. Oh, I just went up 53 points, and I'm Okay. Pretty close to it.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tim has actively monitored his credit score and improved it by 53 points specifically to qualify for this program. This is a high-commitment signal — he bought a house, tracked his credit, and called back. Carter responds with 'You're good. Yeah. Take your time.' — functional but flat. The 53-point improvement is a trust-building anchor Carter could have used to validate Tim's effort and deepen his investment in the appointment.

key_moments.quote: No. It's it that'll work. That'll be good.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter accepts 669 against his own stated threshold of 670 and immediately pivots to scheduling. The booking proceeds on a credit number that is at the edge of — or one point below — the stated policy threshold. The closer is not warned. This is a conditional booking in the sense that the credit qualification is unresolved at the setter stage and will surface again at the proposal.
